source_paper_title: Shapley–Scarf Housing Markets: Respecting Improvement, Integer Programming, and Kidney Exchange
source_paper_bibtex: @article{Bir__2024, title={Shapley–Scarf Housing Markets: Respecting Improvement, Integer Programming, and Kidney Exchange}, volume={49}, ISSN={1526-5471}, url={http://dx.doi.org/10.1287/moor.2022.0092}, DOI={10.1287/moor.2022.0092}, number={3}, journal={Mathematics of Operations Research}, publisher={Institute for Operations Research and the Management Sciences (INFORMS)}, author={Biró, Péter and Klijn, Flip and Klimentova, Xenia and Viana, Ana}, year={2024}, month=aug, pages={1938–1972} }
context:
Conclusion, page 34, Section 6

As the main open question we left open whether the respecting improvement property with regard to the best allotment holds for a) the core for unbounded exchanges b) for stable matchings in the roommates problem with strict preferences.
title: Respecting improvement for best core allotments in unbounded Shapley-Scarf markets
background_latex:
A (Shapley--Scarf) housing market consists of a finite set of agents/objects \(N=\{1,\dots,n\}\). Agent \(i\in N\) is initially endowed with object \(e_i=i\) and has complete and transitive (weak) preferences \(R_i\) over \(N\). Write \(P_i\) for the strict part and \(I_i\) for indifference.

An allocation is a permutation \(x=(x_i)_{i\in N}\in N^N\) such that each agent receives exactly one object and each object is assigned to exactly one agent. An allocation \(x\) is individually rational if \(x_i R_i i\) for all \(i\).

A nonempty coalition \(S\subseteq N\) blocks an allocation \(x\) if there exists an allocation \(z\) using only objects of \(S\) (i.e., \(\{z_i:i\in S\}=S\)) such that \(z_i P_i x_i\) for all \(i\in S\). The (weak) core \(C(R)\) is the set of allocations that are not blocked by any coalition.

An "improvement for agent \(i\)" is a change of preferences from \(R\) to \(\widetilde R\) such that: (1) \(\widetilde R_i=R_i\); (2) for each other agent \(j\neq i\), object \(i\) can only move weakly up in \(j\)'s ranking among acceptable objects (relative orderings among objects \(\neq i\) are unchanged).

For a set of allocations \(X\) and agent \(i\), define the set of objects \(X_i:=\{x_i: x\in X\}\). Define \(\mathrm{best}_i(X)\) as an \(R_i\)-maximal element of \(X_i\) (i.e., an agent \(i\)'s most preferred allotment attainable within \(X\); if there are ties, \(\mathrm{best}_i(X)\) may be non-unique).

The paper proves that the unique competitive/strong-core allocation respects improvement under strict preferences, and gives related setwise results for competitive allocations under weak preferences, but leaves open a question for the (weak) core under unbounded exchanges.
problem_definition_latex:
Given a housing market \((N,R)\) with unbounded exchange cycles and its core \(C(R)\), and an agent \(i\in N\), consider a preference change to \((N,\widetilde R)\) where \(\widetilde R\) is an improvement for \(i\) with respect to \(R\).

Determine whether the following property holds: for every choice of \(x\in C(R)\) with \(x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(R))\) and every choice of \(\widetilde x\in C(\widetilde R)\) with \(\widetilde x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(\widetilde R))\), one has
\[
\widetilde x_i\; R_i\; x_i.
\]
Equivalently, decide whether agent \(i\)'s most preferred allotment among core allocations can ever become strictly worse after \(i\)'s object becomes (weakly) more desirable to other agents (with \(i\)'s own preferences fixed).

Given a housing market \((N,R)\) with unbounded exchange cycles and its core \(C(R)\), and an agent \(i\in N\), consider a preference change to \((N,\widetilde R)\) where \(\widetilde R\) is an improvement for \(i\) with respect to \(R\).

open_problem_quote: As the main open question we left open whether the respecting improvement property with regard to the best allotment holds for a) the core for unbounded exchanges b) for stable matchings in the roommates problem with strict preferences.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: The question asks whether a monotonicity property always holds; a violation would be witnessed by a finite market with explicit preference lists. One can systematically enumerate small markets and check the core before/after an improvement using existing algorithms or IP formulations, making automated counterexample search very feasible.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate objects are concrete preference profiles and an improvement operation; fitness can be the size of the violation (rank drop in the improving agent's best core allotment) computed by solving for the core via IP/TTC-based routines. An evolutionary loop can efficiently explore preference perturbations and market sizes to discover counterexamples or build evidence for validity.

verification.notice: Generated from the paper text only and not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis:
Biró–Klijn–Klimentova–Viana (2021) introduced “respecting improvement” for Shapley–Scarf housing markets and proved it for the unique competitive/strong-core allocation under strict preferences (via TTC), and obtained setwise monotonicity for competitive allocations under weak preferences. They also showed that once exchanges are bounded (e.g., cycle length \(\le 3\)), even strict-preference cores can fail to respect improvement for an agent’s best attainable allotment, leaving open the unbounded-cycle question for the (weak) core: can \(\mathrm{best}_i(C(R))\) become worse after a \(p\)-improvement (others rank \(i\)’s endowment weakly higher, \(R_i\) fixed)?

This open problem has been solved affirmatively by Schlotter–Biró–Fleiner in “The core of housing markets from an agent's perspective: Is it worth sprucing up your home?”. Their Theorem 2 gives a strong monotonicity: starting from any core allocation \(X\in C(H)\), after a \(p\)-improvement \(H'\) there exists \(X'\in C(H')\) with \(X'(p)=X(p)\) or \(X'(p)\,P_p\,X(p)\). Taking \(X\) to be a \(p\)-best core allocation in \(H\) implies that \(p\)’s best attainable core object in \(H'\) is weakly better, yielding the exact quantified statement posed in the source paper: for any \(x\in C(R)\) with \(x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(R))\) and any \(\widetilde x\in C(\widetilde R)\) with \(\widetilde x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(\widetilde R))\), one has \(\widetilde x_i\,R_i\,x_i\). The result holds in full generality of unbounded exchange cycles and is constructive (linear time).

Related subsequent work develops broader “respecting improvement” frameworks for mechanisms (single-valued rules) and for stronger stability notions (e.g., strong core under partial orders), but these do not substitute for the above core-correspondence comparative statics. A remaining direction, suggested already by Biró et al. (2021), is to understand analogous RI-best statements in other stability models (e.g., stable roommates) and to delineate precisely which bounded-exchange restrictions reintroduce failures of monotonicity, as seen in their bounded-cycle counterexamples.

sources.title: The core of housing markets from an agent's perspective: Is it worth sprucing up your home?
sources.theorem_or_result: Theorem 2 (Schlotter–Biró–Fleiner, 2024): For any housing market \(H\), any agent \(p\), any \(p\)-improvement \(H'\) of \(H\), and any core allocation \(X\in C(H)\), there exists a core allocation \(X'\in C(H')\) such that either \(X'(p)=X(p)\) or \(X'(p)\,P_p\,X(p)\). Moreover, such an \(X'\) can be found in \(O(|H|)\) time. Corollary 1: the core strongly satisfies the RI-best property, i.e., for any \(p\)-improvement \(H'\) of \(H\), every \(a\in \Phi_p^+(H)\) and \(a'\in \Phi_p^+(H')\) satisfy \(a\preceq_p a'\) (equivalently \(a'\,R_p\,a\)).
sources.connection: The open problem asks whether, under an improvement for agent \(i\) (others rank object \(i\) weakly higher, \(i\)'s own preferences fixed), agent \(i\)'s best attainable core allotment can become worse: for any \(x\in C(R)\) with \(x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(R))\) and any \(\tilde x\in C(\tilde R)\) with \(\tilde x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(\tilde R))\), must \(\tilde x_i\,R_i\,x_i\) hold? This paper proves exactly this monotonicity for the core (their RI-best property) in full generality, even allowing partial orders and unbounded exchange cycles. Corollary 1 gives the setwise statement about best core outcomes across markets \(H\) and its \(p\)-improvement \(H'\), which matches the quantification over all best-core selections in the open problem. Thus the answer is ‘yes’: best core allotments cannot deteriorate after such an improvement.

sources.title: The core of housing markets from an agent's perspective: Is it worth sprucing up your home?
sources.theorem_or_result: Theorem 2 (Schlotter–Biró–Fleiner, 2024): For any housing market \(H\), any agent \(p\), any \(p\)-improvement \(H'\) of \(H\) (i.e., only \(p\)'s house moves weakly up in other agents’ rankings, with all other relative comparisons unchanged), and any core allocation \(X\in C(H)\), there exists a core allocation \(X'\in C(H')\) such that either \(X'(p)=X(p)\) or \(X'(p)\succ_p X(p)\). Moreover, such an \(X'\) can be found in \(O(|H|)\) time.
sources.connection:
The open problem asks whether, after an improvement for agent \(i\), the agent’s best attainable core allotment can become worse: for any \(x\in C(R)\) with \(x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(R))\) and any \(\tilde x\in C(\tilde R)\) with \(\tilde x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(\tilde R))\), must \(\tilde x_i\,R_i\,x_i\) hold?
- Theorem 2 implies the RI-best property for the core: for any \(x\in C(R)\) there exists some \(\tilde x\in C(\tilde R)\) with \(\tilde x_i\,R_i\,x_i\). Applying this with \(x\) chosen so that \(x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(R))\) shows that \(\mathrm{best}_i(C(\tilde R))\) contains an object weakly preferred to \(x_i\).
- Therefore it is impossible for every best core allotment after the improvement to be strictly worse than a best core allotment before the improvement; equivalently, the agent’s best achievable core object cannot deteriorate.
- In fact, this yields the stronger quantifier pattern in the open problem: if there exists \(\tilde x\in C(\tilde R)\) with \(\tilde x_i\succeq_i x_i\), then any \(\tilde x\) with \(\tilde x_i\in \mathrm{best}_i(C(\tilde R))\) must satisfy \(\tilde x_i\succeq_i x_i\) (since it is at least as good as that witness). Hence the stated property holds.

sources.title: Respecting improvement in markets with indivisible goods
sources.theorem_or_result:
Main strict-domain implication: for generalized matching problems, any rule 
\(\phi: P^N\to M^f\) that satisfies individual rationality, strategy-proofness, and non-bossiness also satisfies respecting improvement (Theorem 1): for any agent \(k\) whose endowment improves in some other agent’s ranking (a \(k\)-improvement), \(\phi_k(R'_i,R_{-i})\,R_k\,\phi_k(R)\). Corollary: extending such a strict-domain rule to weak preferences via fixed tie-breaking preserves respecting improvement on \(R^N\).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns the weak core correspondence \(C(R)\) in (possibly unbounded-cycle) Shapley–Scarf markets and asks whether agent \(i\)’s best attainable core object (i.e., \(\mathrm{best}_i(C(R))\)) is monotone under an improvement of object \(i\) in others’ rankings. This paper does not analyze the core correspondence’s comparative statics, nor does it prove monotonicity for \(\mathrm{best}_i(C(R))\). Instead it studies respecting improvement for single-valued mechanisms/rules (not correspondences) and shows a general sufficient condition (IR+SP+NB) on the strict domain, with extensions to weak preferences via tie-breaking. It also discusses competitive allocations and (strong/weak) core sets in housing markets, but only derives ‘optimistic’ setwise statements for competitive allocations (and notes impossibility of satisfying such best-type properties simultaneously for multiple agents). Thus it provides tools and context but does not resolve the best-core-allotment question.

sources.title: The strong core of housing markets with partial order preferences
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper’s main result on improvements is Theorem 5.4: if \(H=(N,\{\succ_i\})\) has partial-order preferences and \(H'\) is a \(p\)-improvement of \(H\) (i.e., only other agents’ rankings of \(p\)’s house move weakly upward, with all other comparisons unchanged), then for any \(X\in SC(H)\) and \(X'\in SC(H')\) we have \(X'(p)\succeq_p X(p)\). It also gives a structural characterization (Theorem 3.5) and a polynomial-time algorithm (Theorem 4.4) for finding a strong-core allocation under partial orders (with forbidden/forced arcs).
sources.connection: The open problem asks about the (weak) core \(C(R)\) under weak preferences and unbounded exchange cycles: whether agent \(i\)’s best attainable core object can become worse after an improvement for \(i\) (others value \(i\)’s object more). This paper does not address the weak core; it studies the strong core \(SC\) (no weakly blocking cycle) under partial orders and proves a respecting-improvement property for \(SC\) (Theorem 5.4). While conceptually related (same monotonicity question under ‘improvement’), the result does not imply the desired statement for \(C(R)\), because \(SC\subseteq C\) and the best-in-core comparison in the open problem is specific to \(C\) and to selecting \(R_i\)-maximal elements of \(C\). Thus it provides supporting techniques/insight but not a solution to the open problem.

sources.title: Novel integer programming models for the stable kidney exchange problem
sources.theorem_or_result: The paper gives integer programming formulations to compute maximum-cardinality stable and strongly stable exchanges in kidney exchange graphs with bounded cycle/chain length. In particular, it encodes stability via constraints equivalent to: for every cycle \(c\) there exists an arc \((i,j)\in A(c)\) such that \(j\) is matched in the solution to some \(k\) with \(k\) weakly preferred to \(i\) (Proposition 1), and analogous constraints for strong stability under strict/weak preferences (Propositions 2–3).
sources.connection: The open problem concerns a monotonicity/respecting-improvement property for an agent’s best (weak) core allotment in an unbounded Shapley–Scarf housing market under an ‘improvement’ change (others rank object \(i\) weakly higher). This paper does not study that monotonicity question, nor best-core selections, nor unbounded exchange cycles in the Shapley–Scarf model. It instead focuses on computational IP models for finding maximum-cardinality stable/strongly stable exchanges in kidney exchange with typically bounded cycle/chain lengths, and discusses trade-offs between stability and cardinality. While it cites the respecting-improvement property for the TTC outcome in the unbounded case as motivation, it provides no theorem about how core outcomes change under improvements, and no counterexample or proof for the best-core respecting-improvement question.
